God gives Daniel a foresight of the destruction of other kingdoms, which in their day were as powerful as that of Babylon. If we could foresee the changes that will happen when we are gone, we would be less affected by changes in our own time. The ram with two horns was the second empire, that of Media and Persia. He saw this ram overcome by a he-goat. This was Alexander the Great. Alexander, when about thirty-three years old, and at his full strength, died, and showed how empty worldly honor and power are, and that they cannot make a person happy. While people argue, as in the case of Alexander, about the death of some successful warrior, it is clear that the great First Cause of all had no more for him to do, and therefore cut him off. Instead of that one great horn, four notable horns came up, Alexander's four chief captains. A little horn became a great persecutor of the church and people of God. It seems that the Mohammedan delusion is here pointed out. It prospered, and at one time nearly destroyed the holy religion God’s right hand had planted. It is just with God to deprive those of the privileges of his house who despise and profane them; and to make those know the worth of ordinances by the lack of them, who would not know it by enjoying them. Daniel heard the time of this calamity limited and determined, but not when it should come. If we want to know the mind of God, we must apply to Christ, in whom are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge; not hidden from us, but hidden for us. There is much difficulty as to the precise time here stated, but the end of it cannot be very far off. God will, for his own glory, see to the cleansing of the church in due time. Christ died to cleanse his church; and he will cleanse it so that he can present it without fault to himself.

The eternal Son of God stood before the prophet in the appearance of a man, and directed the angel Gabriel to explain the vision. Daniel’s fainting and astonishment at the prospect of evils coming on his people and the church confirm the opinion that long-continued calamities were foretold. The vision being ended, a charge was given to Daniel to keep it private for the present. He kept it to himself, and went on to do the duty of his place. As long as we live in this world we must have something to do in it; and even those whom God has most honored, must not think themselves above their business. Nor must the pleasure of communion with God take us from the duties of our callings, but we must abide with God in them. All who are entrusted with public business must discharge their trust honestly; and, amidst all doubts and discouragements, they may, if true believers, look forward to a happy outcome. Thus should we strive to compose our minds for attending to the duties to which each is appointed, in the church and in the world.
